text: The surgical field shows numerous adhesions, with the stomach adhered to the anterior abdominal wall and liver. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes these adhesions to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the retroesophageal space and fundus of the stomach up to the spleen, ensuring not to divide the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon positions the fundus of the stomach behind the esophagus and applies gastrogastric sutures, typical of a Nissen fundoplication. Two sutures secure the fundus to the stomach, and a third suture attaches it to the esophagus to prevent slippage.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum, separating it from the pancreas by removing flimsy adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy. Using a blunt dissector, the surgeon enters the avascular field and divides the duodenum. The jejunum is then measured approximately 100 centimeters from the duodenojejunal flexure.
This is for only biliary efflux, not for the weight loss. Though there will be some unintentional weight loss, which is helpful for her.
The surgeon anastomoses the loop of jejunum to the proximal duodenum using V-lock sutures, ensuring a secure connection with the visible staple line providing support for the anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the duodenum and jejunum, applying a second layer of V-lock sutures to the posterior wall. This technique ensures a watertight seal and flattens the posterior wall to prevent any ridge or shelf formation at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon continues suturing anteriorly along the opening, applying a single layer of anterior sutures to secure the tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates a well-formed anastomosis between the jejunum and the biliary limb. The efferent limb is measured to ensure no reflux occurs. Enterotomies are performed, and the anastomosis is reinforced using a trans-staple technique, followed by suturing to close the enterotomy. The mesenteric defect is addressed.
The surgeon disconnects the biliary limb from the duodenal jejunal anastomosis, separating these structures visibly within the surgical field.
